text: Programming Methodology February 23, 2025 Page 1 of 6 Programming Methodology (Spring 2025) Section Lecture Time and Room Department CSC 340-02 Tuesday 4:00-6:45PM – Burk Hall 352 Computer Science Instructor: Joseph Hui, jhui@sfsu.edu Course Website: All of the course materials will be available through Canvas. http://canvas.sfsu.edu Office Hours: By appointment only (email several times and I’ll schedule a time. Zoom link on Canvas). Prerequisites: • CSC 220: grade of C or better. • CSC 230: grade of C or better. • The prerequisites for this course are strictly enforced for all sections. Concurrent enrollment is not allowed. Textbook: In this class we will be using: Data Abstraction and Problem Solving with C++: Walls and Mirrors, 7th Edition Objectives: Design and architecture high quality and consistent practical applications and solutions, from concept/idea to integration, testing and delivery. Understand, analyze, and utilize appropriate data structures and algorithms for sorting, searching and graphs. Apply ethical computing concepts and practices to solution design and implementation. Tentative Course Outline: • Students will learn and understand the concepts and applications of basic and advanced C++ topics including ADTs, pointer-based implementation of dynamic arrays and linked lists, operator overloading, STLs, inheritance and polymorphism. Students will apply these skills to develop medium sized C++ projects to solve real-world problems. • Students will learn the problem-solving process and describe their solutions to a computational problem in pseudocode. • Students will learn to analyze the time complexity of foundational algorithms such as binary search, insertion sort, selection sort, quick sort, and finding the k-th ranked object. • Recommended C++ IDEs – Microsoft Visual Studio, Eclipse, or IDE of your choice. There are documentation available on Canvas under Resources for installing Visual Studio or Eclipse (requires C++ compiler). • Text editor and command line using GCC or Clang compiler. Communication: • Email is the best way to communicate with me outside of class. • Feel free to send me questions about the software industry or your resume for feedback. • This is a live document; I may make changes to any of these policies and contents at any time during the semester. I will announce changes in class and post them on Canvas. Programming Methodology February 23, 2025 Page 2 of 6 Tentative Schedule: Week Date Pre-Reading Topic Assignments 1 1/28 Appendix K Syllabus, Course Policies Milestones #1-5 C++ Fundamentals Milestone #1 Assigned 2 2/04 Appendix A, B Interlude 1 C++ for Java Programmers Important Themes in Programming C++ Classes 3 2/11 Interlude 2 Chapter 1 Appendix H, I Pointers, Polymorphism, and Memory Allocation Data Abstraction C++ Header Files and Standard Functions Documentation Systems Milestone #1 Due Milestone #2 Assigned 4 2/18 Appendix C Chapters 18, 4 The Unified Modeling Language Dictionaries and Their Implementations Link-Based Implementations 5 2/25 Chapter 2, Interludes 3, 4 Recursion Exceptions Class Relationships and Reuse Milestone #2 Due Milestone #3 Assigned 6 3/04 Chapters 10, 13 Algorithm Efficiency, Queues and Priority Queues Midterm I review 7 3/11 Midterm I 8 3/18 Interludes 7, 8 The Standard Template Library (I8) Smart Pointers (I4) Midterm I review Milestone #3 Due Milestone #4 Assigned 9 3/25 No Class (Spring Break) 10 4/01 Interlude 5, Chapter 15 Introduce Design Patterns Overloaded Operators and Friend Access Trees 11 4/08 Chapters 11, 16 Sorting Algorithms and Their Efficiency Tree Implementations Discuss Milestone #4 12 4/15 Chapter 17, 19 Heaps Balanced Search Trees Milestone #4 Due Milestone #5 Assigned 13 4/22 Chapter 20 Graphs Midterm II Review 14 4/29 Midterm II 15 5/06 Chapter 21 Processing Data in External Storage Milestone #5 Due Milestone #6 Assigned (#6 Optional) 16 5/13 Guest Lecturer Final Review 17 5/20 Final Exam Milestone #6 Due Programming Methodology February 23, 2025 Page 3 of 6 Grading Policy: 1. Five Milestones 50% a. Milestone 1 (10%) C++ IDE installation and C++ Doubly Linked List b. Milestone 2 (10%) C++ Hashtable c. Milestone 3 (10%) C++ Cache Manager d. Milestone 4 (10%) C++ Binary Search Tree e. Milestone 5 (10%) C++ Cache Manager with Binary Search Tree 2. Midterm Exam I and II: 25% each 3. Final Exam (optional): 25% - best two (2) grades from Midterm I, Midterm II and Final Important Dates: 1. Midterm I Exam: a. Tuesday, Mar 11, 4:00 pm 2. Midterm II Exam a. Tuesday, Apr 29, 4:00 pm 3. Final Exam: a. Tuesday, May 20, 5:00 pm Deadlines: All the assignments will be due on Thursdays at 4:00pm. Please read the course policies to learn more about the policies for checkpoint assignments in this course. Lab Times: Thursday, 4pm-6pm, in TH 434 (Optional – TA is Tyler Baxter, tbaxter1@sfsu.edu) Programming Methodology February 23, 2025 Page 4 of 6 Grades: The final letter grade for the course will be computed using the following distribution: Highest Lowest Letter Grade 100 93 A 92.99 90 A- 89.99 87 B+ 86.99 83 B 82.99 80 B- 79.99 77 C+ 76.00 73 C 72.99 70 C- 69.99 67 D+ 66.99 63 D 62.99 60 D- 59.99 0 F Programming Methodology February 23, 2025 Page 5 of 6 Course Policy: • Attendance: Students are expected to attend all scheduled lectures in this course. Attendance is vital for understanding course material, participating in class discussions, and achieving academic success. Students are responsible for what they miss if they don’t attend any of the scheduled lectures • Exams: All the exams in this course will be given on paper in the classroom and will be proctored by the instructor. A government photo ID (student ID or driver’s license) is required when turning an exam. If any of the scheduled exam dates conflict with your religious observances, you must notify your instructor during the first two weeks of the semester. Drop and withdrawal from courses are student responsibilities. Since two best scores out of three test scores are used to determine the final grade, no makeup exams are allowed (i.e., you can miss a Midterm but will then need to take the final). • Milestones Assignments: This class has four mandatory milestones assignments. The following are their policies: o Only assignments uploaded to GitHub or Canvas will be considered for grading. o All assignments are due on the designated due date, as specified by the instructor, and it is the student’s responsibility to keep track of all assignment due dates and plan accordingly. o Students are responsible for what they turn in. If you submit a blank assignment, it will be graded as such. Always double check your submissions to make sure what you meant to upload is actually what you uploaded. o Late homework submissions will be subject to penalties. A 10% deduction will be applied for each day that the assignment is overdue, up to a maximum of 7 days. After 7 days, the assignment will be considered as not submitted and will be graded as such (i.e., given a 0). This policy applies only to milestones 1, 2, 3, 4 and 5. It is imperative for students to adhere to the specified deadlines, as failing to do so will result in a reduction of their assignment scores. o This policy is designed to emphasize the importance of timely and responsible submission of coursework, while also providing a reasonable window for unforeseen circumstances. Students are strongly encouraged to manage their time effectively to avoid any unnecessary deductions and to ensure the best possible academic outcomes. o Exceptions to this policy must be accompanied by documentation. For instance, if you had Covid or other health issue, then you will need to supply a doctor’s note. • Class Notes: Students may not capture audio, photos or video from class sessions on their own devices without the explicit written permission of the instructor and everyone present, unless part of a DPRC- authorized accommodation. • Office Hours Policies: Office hours are a valuable resource provided by instructors to support students’ learning and offer additional assistance outside of regular lectures. They are intended to foster a deeper understanding of course materials, answer specific questions, provide guidance on assignments, and address any concerns related to the course. It is important to emphasize that office hours are not intended to serve as a replacement for missed lectures. Instructors do not use office hours to re-lecture or repeat the content covered during a missed class. • Instructor Availability: I will be unable to respond emails or messages during weekends/holidays. You can still send an email or message asking for help, but I will be unable to provide a response to your request until the next business day • Final Grades: I create a lot of opportunities for students to do well in this course, and all posted grades are final unless there was a verifiable mistake in the grade calculation. Fairness demands, and university rules, require that all students be marked according to the same standards, so that all students receive the marks they earned. I can’t just raise your final grade for unjustifiable reasons, so I will not respond to any such requests. Programming Methodology February 23, 2025 Page 6 of 6 • Academic Integrity: Any form of cheating or plagiarism will incur very serious consequences. Students may not post any course materials to any third-party sites (such as Chegg) or post any recordings, screenshots, audio or chat transcripts in any setting outside the class, and that violations of this are subject to student disciplinary action. Any instances of cheating, deceit, fabrication, forgery, plagiarism, unauthorized altering of records or submitting false documents, unauthorized collaboration, unauthorized submission of work previously given credit, or other forms of academic misconduct will be assigned a grade penalty, likely an F or a grade of zero. Failing one or more assignments or examinations for reasons of academic integrity violations may result in a final class grade of F. Students may not withdraw from classes in which they have committed academic misconduct. Consequences for violations of academic integrity may exceed an F on the assignment, examination, or class as determined by the Academic Integrity Review Committee. Members of our academic community have a responsibility to develop an awareness of academic integrity, to cultivate skills to realize honesty in academic and community work, and to sustain actively academic honor as a core value of our community. Students are expected to engage in behaviors that reflect well upon the university. In addition to attending to one’s own actions, the Standards for Student Conduct require that students who witness academic dishonesty notify their faculty/instructor, department chair, or the Office of Student Conduct. Supporting academic integrity enhances the reputation of the University and the value attributed to degrees awarded by the University. To learn more about the university policies regarding academic integrity: https://conduct.sfsu.edu/academic-dishonesty. • Students with disabilities: Students with disabilities who need reasonable accommodations are encouraged to contact the instructor. The Disability Programs and Resource Center (DPRC) is available to facilitate the reasonable accommodations process. The DPRC is located in the Student Service Building and can be reached by telephone (voice 415-338-2472, video phone 415-335-7210) or by email (dprc@sfsu.edu). • SF State fosters a campus free of sexual violence including sexual harassment, domestic violence, dating violence, stalking, and/or any form of sex or gender discrimination. If you disclose a personal experience as an SF State student, the course instructor is required to notify the Title IX Coordinator by completing the report form available at http://titleix.sfsu.edu, emailing vpsaem@sfsu.edu or calling 338- 2032. To disclose any such violence confidentially, contact: The SAFE Place - (415) 338-2208; http://www.sfsu.edu/~safe_plc/; Counseling and Psychological Services Center - (415) 338-2208; http://psyservs.sfsu.edu/; For more information on your rights and available resources: http://titleix.sfsu.edu • The Religious and Cultural Holidays Policy (S09-212) states that the faculty of San Francisco State University shall accommodate students wishing to observe religious and cultural holidays when such observances require students to be absent from class activities. It is the responsibility of the student to inform the instructor, in writing, about such holidays during the first two weeks of the class each semester. If such holidays occur during the first two weeks of the semester, the student must notify the instructor, in writing, at least three days before the date that they will be absent. It is the responsibility of the instructor to make every reasonable effort to honor the student request without penalty, and of the student to make up the work missed. • Academic calendar policy: The Academic Calendar can be found at https://webapps.sfsu.edu/public/webcal/acadcalendar
